##### Type
Isotonic crystalloid solution of 0.9% sodium chloride. 
 Contains 151mMol sodium and 151mMol chloride per litre [no schedule] 

##### Presentations
500 or 1000ml of 0.9% sodium chloride solution

##### Actions

- Short acting plasma volume expander

- Also expands interstitial fluid volume

#### Uses

- Initial replacement of fluid, in volume depleted or dehydrated patients. (Volume depletion may be due to loss of blood, plasma or fluid and electrolytes)

- Maintenance of hydration during prolonged patient contact time

- To keep vein open, as IV route for drugs

#### Adverse Effects
Fluid overload 

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Safe to use

##### Breastfeeding
- Safe to use

#### Doses##### Adult
- **In general, aim to maintain sBP ≥90mmHg: **rate and volume infused is dependent on patient condition. Give 250-500mL boluses, reassess after each bolus.
- **Haemorrhagic hypovolaemia: **aggressive warm fluids. 
- Max. dose: 20ml/kg

- **Traumatic brain injury: **aim for sBP ≥100mmHg 
- No limit on amount - dependent on condition of patient. 
- **TKVO: **10 drops per minute (30ml/h with standard drip set)##### Paediatric
- **Refer to paediatric reference charts for BP goals: **rate and volume infused is dependent on patient condition. Give weight appropriate boluses, reassess after each bolus.
- **Paediatric cardiac arrest: **20ml/kg- **Haemorrhagic hypovolaemia: **aggressive warm fluids 
- Max dose: 20ml/kg
- **TKVO: **10 drops per minute (30ml/h with standard drip set)

##### Type
Opioid analgesic [S8]

##### Presentations
10mg in 1ml – glass ampoule

##### Actions

- Analgesia

- Anxiolytic

- Vasodilation

##### Pharmacokinetics
**IV**
 Onset: 5 - 10 minutes
 Max Effect: 20 minutes
 Duration: 4 - 5 hours

**IM**
 Onset: 10 - 30 minutes
 Max Effect: 30 minutes
 Duration: 4 - 5 hours

#### Uses

- Analgesia for moderate to severe pain

- Maintain analgosedation in intubated patients in conjunction with midazolam.

- Reduce dyspnoea in palliative care patients.

- Relieve suffering due to terminal palliative care emergencies (no active treatment).

#### Adverse Effects

- Nausea and vomiting

- Altered level of consciousness

- Respiratory depression

- Hypotension

- Bradycardia

#### Contraindications

- Respiratory depression

- Systolic blood pressure Disregard contraindications when managing palliative care emergencies (no active treatment) patients.

#### Precautions

- GCS ≤13

- Known hypersensitivity

- Frail patients

- COPD patients

- Hypovolaemic patients

- Systolic BP 70 – 90mmHg (see below)

- Children **AP:** max. total dose 20mg
- **ICP:** no max. total dose
- Consider IM: 
- IV/IO unobtainable
- Little risk of hypotension- Patient contact estimated >20/60- No ischaemic chest pain- 0.1mg/kg IM 
- Repeat once @30/60 if required
- **AP:** max. total dose 20mg##### Adults only
IV/IO morphine may be administered to patients with mild-moderate hypotension (sBP 70 – 90mmHg)

- 0.025mg/kg IV/IO over 60 seconds. 
- Repeat @10/60 as required.
- Do not repeat if sBP drops ≥10mmHg, even if it remains above 70mmHg. 
- **AP:** max. total dose 20mg- **ICP:** no max. total dose#### Post-intubation analgosedation (as adjunct to midazolam)##### Adult (infusion pump)

##### Type
Benzodiazepine 

##### Presentations
15mg in 3mL ampoule

##### Actions

- Antiepileptic

- Sedative

- Anxiolytic

- Amnesic

##### Pharmacokinetics
**IV/IO**
 Onset: 2 minutes
 Max Effect: 3 - 5 minutes
 Duration: 7 - 75 minutes

**IM**
 Onset: 5 - 15 minutes
 Max Effect: 30 - 60 minutes
 Duration: 2 - 6 hours

#### Uses

- Status epilepticus

- Post intubation analgosedation in conjunction with morphine

- Relieve distress or restlessness in the palliative care patient

- Relieve suffering due to terminal palliative care emergencies (no active treatment)

#### Adverse Effects

- Hypotension
More common when administer via fast IV push

- Respiratory depression and loss of airway
More common at high doses or when synergising with other CNS depressant substances

#### Contraindications

- Previous allergic reaction to Midazolam

#### Precautions

- Hepatic failure

- Frail or elderly

- Known or suspected concurrent CNS depressant use

- Known or suspected haemodynamic compromise

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- **Approved** for administration in ACTAS setting.

##### Breastfeeding
- **Approved** for administration in ACTAS setting.

#### Doses#### Status Epilepticus##### Adult and Paediatric
- 0.2mg/kg IM (max. 10mg) 
- Repeat @5/60 if required. (max. 2 doses)

- **ICP:** 0.2mg/kg IV/IO push over 60 sec. (max. 10mg) 
- Repeat @5/60 if required. (max. 2 doses)
#### Post Intubation Sedation (as adjunct to ketamine)##### Adult and Paediatric
- **ICP:** 0.05mg/kg IV/IO push over 60 sec. 
- Repeat @5/60 as required.

#### Post Intubation Sedation (as adjunct to morphine)##### Adult
**Note:** Withdraw 29mL from 50mL NaCl bag, then add 6mL (30mg) midazolam and 3mL (30mg) morphine to make 30mg:30mg/30mL

- **ICP:** Initial infusion rate: 5mg/hr 
- Lower soft infusion limit: 1mg/hr
- Upper soft infusion limit: 30mg/hr
- Upper hard infusion limit: 50mg/hr- **ICP:** Default bolus: 2mg (2mL) 
- Lower hard bolus limit: 0.5mg (0.5mL)
- Upper hard bolus limit: 5mg (5mL)#### Palliative Care
**Note:** Always refer to and follow patient specific treatment plan when available.
##### Adult with distress or restlessness
- 2.5 – 5mg SC/IM
Consider patient weight when selecting dose
##### Adult with terminal palliative care emergency
- 5mg SC/IM/IV
In conjunction with morphine
- Repeat @10/60 if requires (max. 60mg)

##### Paediatric
Refer to patient specific treatment plan for all palliative care presentations.

##### Type
A naturally occurring catecholamine [S3]

##### Presentations
1:10,000 – 1mg in 10ml – glass ampoule 
 1:1,000 – 1mg in 1ml – glass ampoule 

##### Actions

- \u03b1 effect: peripheral vasoconstriction

- \u03b21 effects: 

- increased rate of sinus node

- increased myocardial contractility

- increased AV conduction

- increased myocardial irritability

- \u03b22 effects: 

- bronchodilation

- vasodilation of skeletal muscle

Onset: IV = 30 seconds; IM = 30 – 90 seconds 
 Max effect: IV = 3 – 5 min; IM = 4 – 10 mins 

Endotracheal use: slightly longer times

#### Uses

- Cardiac arrest

- Anaphylaxis

- Severe life-threatening asthma

#### Indications
- Patients requiring an adrenaline infusion.

#### Procedure

- Medication checks are to be done by two officers prior to administration.

- Adhere to all aseptic techniques.

- Inject **1mg of adrenaline into a 500ml** bag of saline via the access port.

- Fill out and attach an additive label to the fluid bag.

- Connect a giving set or a burette to the fluid bag. 
- **If you are using a giving set:** ensure the gate clamps are turned off prior to inserting into the fluid bag.
- **If you are using a burette:** connect a giving set to the bottom of the burette and ensure all gate clamps are turned off prior to inserting into the fluid bag. Now open the ventilation port then the gate clamp and fill the burette to the 100ml mark. Open the gate clamp and prime the line and then refill the burette to the 100ml mark.

- Connect the giving set to the patient and commence infusion. 
- 20 drops/minute = 1ml per minute or 2 mcg/minute
- Titrate for a response.

- Reassess respiratory and perfusion status.

#### Contraindications
- Nil when indicated.

#### Specific Notes
- Ensure that all of the delivery lines are primed with adrenaline. Failure to do this will result in a in a significant delay in the patient receiving adrenaline.
- If the adrenaline infusion is running through an IO line, you may require a pump set rather than a giving set to overcome increased intra medullar pressure.

#### Procedure

- Place strip into monitor. The glucometer will auto turn on.

- Select a finger and clean with an alcohol swab, allow finger to dry.

- Using a lancet, pierce the skin and squeeze a small amount of blood from the wound.

- Dispose of the used lancet appropriately.

- Place a sample of blood on the test strip until the BGL device beeps and a countdown appears.

- Apply a Band-Aid to the wound.

- Wait for the test result and record the BGL measurement.

- Dispose of the used test strip appropriately.

#### Specific Notes

Amiodarone.text: Formula: 5mg/kg Preparation: 150mg in 3mls Dose: 300mg Volume: 6ml Volume: 6ml Formula: 50mg/kg Preparation: 2.5g in 5mls Dose: 2.5g Volume: 5ml

##### Type
Potent anti-arrhythmic agent [S4]

##### Presentations
150mg in 3ml – glass ampoule

##### Actions
Complex electrophysiological and pharmacological profile:

- Prolongs the action potential duration; increases the refractoriness of all cardiac tissues

- Blocks Na+ channels (class I action)

- Has some anti-adrenergic effects (class II action)

- Ca2+ blockade (class IV action)

- Prolongs QT interval – reflects global prolongation of repolarisation 

- When given IV, there is a significant effect on the AV node which causes a delay in nodal conduction

- Also effective for accessory pathway conduction 

#### Uses
Effective for both supraventricular and ventricular arrhythmias. ACTAS uses: 

- To slow the ventricular rate in atrial fibrillation and flutter 

- To treat VF and VT

#### Adverse Effects
When given IV, can cause vasodilation, negative inotropic effects and hypotension (dose and rate dependent).

Occasionally may cause:

- Bradycardia (especially in older patients)

- Phlebitis

- Hot flushes / sweating

#### Contraindications

- Known hypersensitivity

- Cross sensitivity to iodine

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Only use in the setting of cardiac arrest or peri-arrest

##### Breastfeeding
- Approved for administration in ACTAS setting, document breastfeeding status in ePCR

- No repeat dose for either treatment regime.
##### Paediatric
- **ICP:** 5mg/kg IV (to maximum 150mg) via Springfusor 
- Made up to 7ml total volume with normal saline (7ml will run over 10 minutes)

- No repeat dose for either treatment regime.
#### Special Notes
- Significant potential drug interactions.
- The following may potentiate the actions of amiodarone: 
- Digoxin
- Phenytoin (Dilantin)
- \u03b2 blockers- Ca2+ channel blocker- Other antiarrhythmics.

Treat with 100% oxygen.

##### Type
A naturally occurring gas 

##### Presentations
Compressed gas in all-white cylinders of various sizes, to be fitted with an appropriate pressure-reducing device 

##### Actions
Essential element for aerobic metabolic needs and sustaining life. 
 Oxygen administration improves, or prevents a reduction in, the oxygen content of blood leaving the lungs. 

#### Uses
A wide range of situations, for the treatment or prevention of hypoxaemia

#### Adverse Effects

- Increased risk of fire / explosion – oxygen strongly supports combustion 

- Mucosal dryness and irritation

- Hypoventilation in some COPD patients with CO2 retention (titrate oxygen to maintain SpO2 88 – 92%)

#### Contraindications

- Known paraquat poisoning with SpO2 ≥88%

- History of bleomycin therapy with SpO2 ≥88%

#### Precautions
Neonates (especially pre-term infants) are particularly susceptible to the toxic effects of oxygen. When absolutely required, the lowest effective concentration, for the shortest possible time, should be used to achieve adequate oxygenation.

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Safe to use

##### Breastfeeding
- Safe to use

#### Doses##### Adult and Paediatric
With the exception of conditions listed below, oxygen should generally be titrated to maintain SpO2 ≥94% (i.e, unless otherwise required, there is no need to push saturations to 100%). 

Deliver oxygen through a device appropriate to the required inspired concentration of oxygen.

- **Nasal cannulae: **1 – 4 litres/min (approx. 24 – 40% oxygen) for low oxygen requirements
- **Oxygen (‘Hudson’) mask: **6 – 15 litres/min (approx. 30 – 65% oxygen) for moderate oxygen requirements
- **Nebuliser: **8 litres/min for moderate oxygen requirements with need for aerosolised medications- **Non-rebreather mask (NRBM): **15 litres/min (approx. 60 – 97% oxygen) for high oxygen requirements- **Bag-valve-mask (BVM): **15 litres/min or demand valve (up to 100% oxygen) for high oxygen requirements- **Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP): **8 – 15 litres/min (titrated to achieve desired effect/pressure) for high oxygen requirements.
#### Conditions with specific oxygen requirement
- Conditions requiring 100% oxygen delivery (NRBM, BVM) 
- Obstetric emergencies
- Carbon monoxide poisoning
- Unrelieved upper airway obstruction
- Diving emergencies.
- COPD patients with possible CO2 retention – titrate oxygen delivery to maintain SpO2 of 88 – 92%.
- Rapid sequence intubation (RSI) – aim for highest SpO2 possible 
- High flow oxygen (15 litres/min) via nasal cannulae is mandatory during this procedure, in addition to IPPV.

#### Indications
- For patients that require supplemental oxygen.

#### Procedure

Figure 1

##### Nasal Prongs (minimal oxygen requirements – approx 24-40%)

- Attach nasal prong tubing to the oxygen port.

- Select oxygen flow rate. 
- **Note: 15 Lpm continuous during intubation.**

- Place one nasal prong in each nostril and tubing tucked behind each ear.

- Position the slide adjuster underneath the patient’s chin.

- Reassess respiratory and perfusion status.

Figure 2

##### Hudson Mask (moderate oxygen requirements – approx 30-65%)

- Attach the oxygen tubing to the Hudson mask.

- Connect the oxygen tubing to the oxygen port.

- Place elastic banding over patients head and adjust as required.

- Position the mask over the patient’s nose and mouth, moulding the metal nose piece if required.

- Reassess respiratory and perfusion status.

Figure 3

##### Non-Rebreather Mask (high oxygen requirements - approx 60-97%)

- Prepare as for Hudson mask.

- Select oxygen flow rate of >10 Lpm.

- Ensure reservoir bag is inflated.

- Place elastic banding over patients head and adjust as required.

- Position the mask over the patient’s nose and mouth, moulding the nose piece if required.

- Reassess respiratory and perfusion status.

Atropine.text: In an MCI situation, it’s permissible to simply repeat the initial atropine dose every 5/60.
If heart rate less than 50/minute, consider atropine prior to suxamethonium.

##### Type
Parasympathetic blocking agent [S4]

##### Presentations
1200mcg in 1ml – plastic ampoule 

##### Actions

- Blocks the action of the vagus nerve on the heart

- Increases the rate of the sinus node

- Increases speed of conduction through the AV node

- Reduces the amount of secretions from some glands (e.g., tear and salivary glands)

Onset IV: 2 minutes. Max effect: 5 minutes.

IV half-life: adult – 2–3 hours; paed – approx. 6.5 hours.

#### Uses

- Bradyarrhythmias with poor perfusion **- ICP only**

- Organophosphate/ nerve agent poisoning

- Big black spider bite with cholinergic symptoms

#### Adverse Effects

- Tachycardia

- Palpitations

- Blurred vision

- Dry mouth

- Confusion

- Urinary retention

- Increased body temperature (by decreasing perspiration)

#### Contraindications
Known hypersensitivity.

#### Precautions

- Care needed in patients with glaucoma

- Aim not to increase heart rate above 100bpm

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Safe to use

##### Breastfeeding
- Safe to use

#### Doses#### Bradyarrhythmias##### Adult
- **ICP:** IV / IO: 600mcg – fast push 
- Repeat once after 3-5 minutes, if required.
##### Paediatric
Not used

#### Organophosphate / Nerve Agent Poisoning##### Adult
- IV / IO / IM: initial dose 2400mcg – fast push
- **AP: ** IM/IV: initial dose 2400mcg – fast push
- Repeat every 3 – 5 minutes.
- Double each repeat dose as required (e.g. 2.4mg → 4.8mg → 9.6mg etc.). - No upper limit on doses until all management end goals achieved.

- **Note:** Give IM while IV/IO access is being obtained.
##### Paediatric
- IV / IO / IM: initial dose 50mcg/kg (max. initial dose 2400mcg) – fast push 
- **AP: ** IM/IV: initial dose 50mcg/kg (max. initial dose 2400mcg) – fast push - Repeat every 3 – 5 minutes.
- Double each repeat dose as required- No upper limit on doses until all management end goals achieved.

- **Note:** Give IM while IV/IO access is being obtained.
- Split larger doses into multiple injections. 
**Note:** In an MCI situation where multiple patients require simultaneous management, a simplified repeat dose regimen, where the initial dose is repeated instead of doubled doses, can be used. 

#### Big black spider bite with cholinergic symptoms##### Adult
- IV / IO / IM: 600mcg – fast push
- **AP: ** IM/IV: 600mcg – fast push
- No repeat##### Paediatric
- IV / IO / IM: 20mcg/kg (max. 600mcg) – fast push
- **AP: ** IM/IV: 20mcg/kg (max. 600mcg) - fast- No repeat

#### Uses

- Relieve acute pulmonary oedema

- Management of autonomic dysreflexia

#### Adverse Effects

- Headache

- Flushing of skin

#### Contraindications

- Do not administer if systolic BP is

Ondansetron.text: Ondansetron may be given in exceptional circumstances, but is not recommended (category B1).

##### Type
Potent anti-nauseant and antiemetic [S4]

##### Presentations
4mg in 2ml – plastic or glass ampoule 
 4mg – orally disintegrating tablet (ODT) 

##### Actions

- Potent, highly specific and selective serotonin 5-HT receptor antagonist. 

- Precise mode of action in control of nausea and vomiting is not known. Likely to have actions peripherally and in the central nervous system.

- Maximum effect is approximately 10 minutes following IV administration. 

- Hepatic metabolism.

#### Uses
For the prevention or treatment of nausea and vomiting

#### Adverse Effects

- Headache

- Flushing of skin

- Occasional reaction at the site of IV injection

- Drowsiness

- Anxiety and agitation

- Transient visual disturbances

#### Contraindications
Known hypersensitivity

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Use only for hyperemesis

##### Breastfeeding
- Safe to use

#### Doses##### Adult
- IV: 8mg slowly over 2 minutes 
- May be administered IM if necessary
- Single dose only.

- ODT: 8mg sublingually 
- Single dose only.
##### Paediatric
- IV: 0.1mg/kg (max. 4mg) 
- Slowly over 2 minutes
- May be administered IM if necessary
- Repeat once if required, after approximately 10 minutes. - ODT: 
- ≥6mths – 3yrs = 2mg (single dose only)
- 4 – 9yrs = 4mg (single dose only)- ≥10yrs = 8mg (single dose only).

Aspirin.text:
##### Type
Non – steroidal anti-inflammatory drug [S2]

##### Presentation
300mg tablet

##### Actions

- Inhibits platelet function (for up to 7 days)

- Suppresses inflammation, reduces fever, relieves pain (rapidly absorbed from the stomach and small bowel)

#### Uses
Suspected myocardial ischaemia

#### Adverse Effects

- Allergic reactions

- Aggravation of bleeding tendencies

- Gastric irritation (unlikely after single dose)

#### Contraindications

- Known or suspected allergy to salicylates

- Known or suspected active bleeding

- Known bleeding tendency

- Current psychostimulant overdose (due to an increased risk of cerebral haemorrhage) 

#### Precautions

- Peptic ulcer

- Patients on anticoagulants

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- 300mg until 32 weeks, then reduce to 75mg

##### Breastfeeding
- 150mg when breastfeeding

#### Doses##### Adult
- 1 tablet (300mg) 
- Chewed and swallowed, or dissolved in a small amount of water. 

- \xbd tablet (150mg) if on warfarin.
#### Special Notes
- Single dose only.
- Administer even when patient is on slow release aspirin.

Adenosine.text: Record a 12 lead ECG prior to use of adenosine.
If patient has previously had an unpleasant (fearful) experience with adenosine, consider a pre-dose of midazolam 1 – 1.5mg IV.

##### Type
Endogenous purine nucleoside, found in all body cells [S4]

##### Presentations
6mg in 2ml – vial

##### Actions
- Causes transient inhibition of conduction in the heart, especially in the AV node.
- Onset: 5-10 seconds
- Duration: approximately 10 seconds

#### Uses

- Treatment of supraventricular tachycardia.

- Not for the treatment of atrial flutter or fibrillation; however, if mistakenly administered to patients in these arrhythmias, the decrease in AV conduction may unmask atrial activity. 

#### Adverse Effects
Common, although transient and generally minor:

- Arrhythmias at the time of conversion are common (up to 55% patients) – including PVCs, PACs, sinus bradycardia, AV blocks

- Transient flushing of the skin

- Mild dyspnoea

- Chest tightness

- Nausea

- Headache

- Feelings of apprehension and fear

#### Contraindications

- 2\xb0 or 3\xb0 heart block

- Known hypersensitivity

#### Precautions

- Asthma – may exacerbate bronchospasm

- Antagonised by: theophylline (Nuelin)

- Potentiated by: 
- dipyridamole (Persantin, Asasantin)
- carbamazepine (Tegretol, Teril)

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Safe to use

##### Breastfeeding
- Safe to use

#### Doses##### Adult (symptomatic adults only)
- **ICP: **6mg IV - rapid bolus (over 1 - 2 seconds) 
- Give into a fast flowing pump set.

- **ICP: **If first is unsuccessful: 
- Give second dose (2 minutes between doses)
- 12mg IV - rapid bolus (over 1 - 2 seconds).##### Paediatric
- **ICP:** First dose: 0.05mg/kg – given as above 
- **ICP:** Second dose: 0.1mg/kg
#### Special Notes
- Use only after Valsalva manoeuvre x 2.
- Record a 12 lead ECG prior to use of adenosine.
- Rapid injection with a pump set increases likelihood of success.
- Elevate limb if possible.
- Even if the arrhythmia is successfully converted, the patient should still be transported to hospital as the incidence of recurrent arrhythmias is quite high (10 – 15%).
- If patient has previously had an unpleasant (fearful) experience with adenosine, consider a pre-dose of midazolam 1 – 1.5mg IV.

Ceftriaxone.text: It is possible that 20% of patients with a penicillin allergy may be sensitive to ceftriaxone

##### Type
3rd generation cephalosporin antibiotic [S4]

##### Presentations
1g ceftriaxone powder for reconstitution – vial

##### Actions

- Broad spectrum antibiotic – especially effective against gram-negative bacteria (e.g., meningococcus)

- Effective crossing of blood-brain barrier

#### Uses
Suspected meningococcal disease, in acutely ill patients

#### Adverse Effects

- Hypersensitivity reactions (not common)

- Diarrhoea

- Skin rash

#### Contraindications
Known sensitivity to cephalosporins

#### Precautions
It is possible that 20% of patients with a penicillin allergy may be sensitive to ceftriaxone

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Safe to use

##### Breastfeeding
- Safe to use

#### Doses##### Adult and Paediatric
- 50mg/kg, to a total of 2g – IV preferred 
- Make up to 10ml with normal saline
- Give slowly over 2 - 4 minutes
- May be administered IM: Reconstitute each 1g vial with 3.5ml lignocaine. Draw up dose into two syringes and administer into two separate injection sites.
- **ICP:** May also be given IO if no IV available. 
#### Special Notes
- An incident report should be submitted to CGU if ceftriaxone is administered.

Lignocaine.text: May be administered IM: Reconstitute each 1g vial with 3.5ml lignocaine. Draw up dose into two syringes and administer into two separate injection sites.

40mg through primed IO extension tubing, slowly over 2 minutes  Allow lignocaine to dwell in IO space for 60 seconds, then flush with 5-10ml of normal saline Repeat after 5 minutes if needed: 20mg over 60 seconds (max. 2 x repeats) Total max. dose 80mg
0.5mg/kg (to max. 40mg) through primed IO extension tubing, slowly over 2 minutes  Allow lignocaine to dwell in IO space for 60 seconds, then flush with 5-10ml of normal saline

In the conscious/semi conscious patient, the port line should be primed with the approved designated dose of Lignocaine. This is to be flushed with 1.2-1.5mls of normal saline in order to push the Lignocaine into the medullary space. Wait 2 to 3mins for Lignocaine to take effect.

##### Type

- Local anaesthetic

- Anti-arrhythmic [S4]

- **High-risk medication**

##### Presentations
50mg in 5ml – plastic ampoule

##### Actions

- Local anaesthetic effects

- Suppresses ventricular arrhythmias

- Onset SCI: 1 – 4 mins. Max effect: 5 – 10 minutes.

- Onset IV: 1 – 3 minutes.

#### Uses

- Local anaesthesia prior to invasive procedures

- VT with cardiac output, in patients who cannot have amiodarone

- Flushing of a painful IO in an aware patient

- Solvent for IM ceftriaxone administration

#### Adverse Effects
These effects are extremely unlikely in usual subcutaneous doses, especially if the syringe is continually aspirated. More likely if given IV.

- CNS effects: 
 stimulation followed by depression, drowsiness, agitation, muscle twitching, seizures, coma 

- Cardiac effects: 
 hypotension, bradycardia, heart block, asystole 

#### Contraindications
Known hypersensitivity 

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Safe to use

##### Breastfeeding
- Safe to use

- **ICP:** Up to 5ml subcutaneously

- **ICP:** 1mg/kg – slow IV / IO over 2 minutes

- **ICP:** 40mg through primed IO extension tubing, slowly over 2 minutes 
- Allow lignocaine to dwell in IO space for 60 seconds, then flush with 5-10ml of normal saline
- Repeat after 5 minutes if needed: 20mg over 60 seconds (max. 2 x repeats)
- Total max. dose 80mg
- Consider systemic pain relief for patients not responding to IO lignocaine##### Paediatric

- Allow lignocaine to dwell in IO space for 60 seconds, then flush with 5-10ml of normal saline

- Consider systemic pain relief for patients not responding to IO lignocaine

Naloxone.text:
##### Type
Narcotic antagonist [S4]

##### Presentations
Varies frequently – check before use

##### Actions
Reverses the effects of narcotic analgesics. 
**IV** – onset: 1 – 2 minutes; duration: 30 – 45 minutes. 
**IM** - timing unknown, but thought to have a slightly longer onset and duration than when given IV. 

#### Uses

- Coma

- Drug overdose and poisoning

- Use in situations where there is significant decrease in level of consciousness; where there is hypoventilation and/or loss of protective reflexes; and where overdose of narcotics cannot be positively excluded 

#### Adverse Effects

- May precipitate acute withdrawal syndrome in narcotic addicts

- Occasional aggressive behaviour following reversal

- Nausea and vomiting

#### Contraindications
Known hypersensitivity

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Approved for administration in ACTAS setting, use in opioid dependant mothers can cause serious withdrawal in foetus, strongly advocate for transport or support service involvement

##### Breastfeeding
- Approved for administration in ACTAS setting, breastfeeding is not recommended within 12-24 hours of a significant narcotic overdose, strongly advocate for transport or support service involvement to ensure child safety

#### Doses##### Adult
- 1st dose: 0.8mg IM 
- Repeat at 5 minutes if required.

- 2nd dose: 0.8mg IM 
**OR**- **ICP:** 2nd and subsequent doses: 
- 0.4mg IV increments, fast push
- May repeat IV dose up to 3 times (to maximum of 2mg)- All doses may be administered IM or IV as the situation demands.##### Paediatric
- IM: 0.01mg/kg 
- Repeat after 2 – 5 minutes if no effect
- Maximum paediatric dose: 3 x calculated doses.
- **ICP:** IV: 0.01mg/kg – fast push 
- Maximum paediatric dose: 3 x calculated doses
- All doses may be administered IM or IV as the situation demands.
#### Special Notes
- When used IV, the effect may wear off rapidly, especially if a large dose of narcotic has been taken. An IM dose is highly recommended if the patient is likely to refuse transport.
- Special care is needed if long-acting agents are known or suspected to have been used (e.g. MS Contin, methadone, etc). In this case, give a larger IM dose and strongly encourage transport to hospital.

Heparin.text: Remember that these patients are often on fluid restrictions. 100-200ml IV fluids will often resolve a hypotensive episode. Consult the patient’s treatment records to ascertain their usual blood pressure.

##### Type
Unfractioned heparin anticoagulant

- **High-risk medication**

##### Presentations
5000u heparin in 1 ml – glass ampoule

##### Actions

- Inhibits blood clotting

- Combines with antithrombin III, a naturally occurring anti-clotting factor in plasma, and inhibits the conversion of prothrombin to thrombin

- It also interferes with the actions of other clotting factors with fibrin stabilising factor

#### Uses
Patients with an acute ST elevation myocardial infarction who meet the requirements for early percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) procedure

#### Adverse Effects

- Bleeding

- Thrombocytopenia

- Anaphylactoid reactions (rare)

#### Contraindications
Refer to heparin checklist

#### Precautions
Use with care in patients with extensive history of allergies

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Approved for administration in ACTAS setting, **contraindicated** during labour

##### Breastfeeding
- Safe to use

#### Doses##### Adult
- 5000u IV – over 30-60 seconds

##### Paediatric
Not used.

#### Special Notes
- This treatment should not delay transport time to hospital.

Hydrocortisone.text:
##### Type
Adrenocorticosteroid [S4]

##### Presentations
100mg powder in 2ml vial (reconstitute with 2ml normal saline)

##### Actions
Numerous and widespread. ACTAS administration for:

- Anti-inflammatory effects on the airways

- Stimulates the normal increase in cortisol secretion in response to stress 

#### Uses

- Moderate to severe respiratory distress due to asthma or exacerbation of COPD 

- Patients with a diagnosed adrenal insufficiency who experience a stress response and exhibit altered conscious state and/or refractory hypotension 

#### Adverse Effects
Nil significant with single use

#### Contraindications
Known previous reaction to corticosteroids

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Safe to use

##### Breastfeeding
- Approved for administration in ACTAS setting, document breastfeeding status in ePCR, advise against breastfeeding for 4 hours post administration 

#### Doses#### Asthma and COPD##### Adult
- **ICP:** 100mg IV – slowly over 2 minutes 

##### Paediatric
- **ICP:** 4mg/kg IV (to a maximum of 100mg) – slowly over 2 minutes 

#### Adrenal crisis##### Adult
- **ICP:** 100mg IV – slowly over 2 minutes 
- May be administered IM.

##### Paediatric
- **ICP:** IV (slowly over 2 minutes): 
- 12 years = 100mg IV.
- **ICP:** May be administered IM.
#### Special Notes
- Hydrocortisone is not a first-line priority drug in the management of severe respiratory distress. It is only to be given after aggressive oxygenation, inhaled bronchodilators, and (where necessary) adrenaline.

Simple analgesic / antipyretic

Methoxyflurane.text: Inhaler to 45\xb0 and carefully pour contents of a single 3ml Methoxyflurane bottle onto the base whilst rotating.

##### Type
Volatile inhalation anaesthetic and analgesic agent [S4]

- **High-risk medication**

##### Presentations
3ml bottle

##### Actions
Central nervous system depressant

Onset: 3 – 5 mins. Offset: 3 – 5 mins.

#### Uses
For relief of pain of all origins

#### Adverse Effects

- Altered consciousness

- Renal dysfunction (rare with once only ambulance use)

- Jaundice (rare with once only ambulance use)

- Uterine atonia

#### Contraindications

- Depressed level of consciousness

- Known hypersensitivity

- Malignant hyperthermia

- Concurrent doxycycline use (e.g. Doryx, Doxsig, Doxylin, Frakas, etc.) 

#### Precautions

- Renal disease

- Diabetes

- Caution if patient unable to self-administer

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- **Contraindicated** if pre-eclampsia is suspected, otherwise, safe to use. If used during labour, discontinue use post-delivery, and if PPH is suspected.

##### Breastfeeding
- Approved for administration in ACTAS setting

#### Doses##### Adult and Paediatric 
- Up to 3ml – self administered via Penthrox inhaler 
- Repeat once if required
- Dose should not exceed 6ml/day or 15ml/week
- If used during labour, discontinue use post-delivery, and if PPH is suspected.

#### Indications
- Patients requiring administration of Methoxyflurane for pain management. 

#### Procedure

- Explain the procedure to the patient.

- Confirm “5 rights” with paramedic partner, **the right**: 
- Patient
- Drug
- Dose
- Time
- Route
- Hold the Methoxyflurane bottle upright, use the base of the Penthrox\xae Inhaler to loosen the cap. Remove and discard the cap.

- Tilt the Penthrox\xae Inhaler to 45\xb0 and carefully pour contents of a single 3ml Methoxyflurane bottle onto the base whilst rotating.

- Place wrist loop over patient’s wrist. Instruct the patient to inhale through the mouthpiece and exhale normally. The first few breaths should be gentle.

- If stronger analgesia is required, patient may cover the dilution hole with their finger.

- Approximately 8-10 breaths are required to achieve any effect of the drug.

#### Considerations
- Do not put more than 3mls in the inhaler at any time, as there is an increased risk of droplet inhalation.
- 3mls should remain active in the inhaler for approximately 30 minutes.
- A resuscitation mask or an oxygen therapy line can be placed on the end of the inhaler if required.
#### Specific Notes
- Always ensure medication pre administration checks are completed prior to any drug administration.

Calcium Chloride.text:
##### Type
Electrolyte – utilised in both electrical and mechanical actions of the myocardium [no schedule]

- **High-risk medication**

##### Presentations
1g in 10ml – glass vial 

##### Actions
Opposes the action of high serum potassium on the myocardium

Onset: several minutes
 Duration: 30 – 90 minutes 

#### Uses

- Emergency treatment of hyperkalaemia

- Cardiac arrest secondary to hyperkalaemia (i.e., hyperkalaemia is thought to be the cause of the cardiac arrest)

- Prolonged hypotension post-magnesium administration, if unresponsive to fluid 

#### Adverse Effects
- Tissue necrosis if extravasation
- Precipitates out in contact with bicarbonate
- If fast pushed: 

- Bradycardia

- Peripheral vasodilation

- Hypotension

#### Contraindications

- Known hypersensitivity

- Digoxin overdose

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Approved for administration in ACTAS setting

##### Breastfeeding
- Approved for administration in ACTAS setting

#### Doses##### Adult and Paediatric 
- **ICP:**** With cardiac output:** 10mg/kg (up to 1g) IV/IO 
- Over 2 minutes
- Repeat once, if required.

- **ICP:**** No cardiac output:** 10mg/kg (up to 1g) IV/IO 
- Over 30 – 60 seconds
- Repeat once, if required.

Fentanyl.text:
##### Type
Synthetic narcotic analgesic [S8]

##### Presentations
100mcg in 2mL (5mcg/0.1mL) glass ampoule

##### Actions

- Precipitates CNS effects by binding to opioid receptors.

##### Pharmacokinetics
IN
 Onset: ~ 5 minutes
 Max Effect: 5 – 15 minutes
 Duration: ~ 60 minutes

#### Uses

- Analgesic for moderate to severe pain.

- Safe alternative analgesic in patients with known morphine allergy.

#### Adverse Effects

- Reduced conscious state

- Respiratory depression

- Nausea/vomiting

- Bradycardia (rare)

- Rigidity of the diaphragm, intercostals and jaw (rare)

#### Contraindications

- Known allergy or hypersensitivity

- Bilateral bleeding or occluded nostrils

- Age Increased risk of adverse effects. Consider reducing dose. 

- Frail or elderly
Increased risk of adverse effects. Consider reducing dose. 

- Reduced level of consciousness
Potential difficulty correctly administering via IN route. 

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Refrain from use if labour suspected. If administered, prepare for neonatal respiratory support and naloxone administration. 

##### Breastfeeding
- May be used in conservative doses. Encourage feeding/expressing prior to administration.

#### Doses#### Analgesia – Intranasal##### Adult and Paediatric
- 1.5mcg/kg IN, up to 100mcg. 
- Repeat @5/60 intervals as required.

#### Further Information
- The atomiser dead space is 0.1mL. To account for this, an additional 0.1mL must be drawn up with the first administration. Pragmatically, this is not required when delivering the full 100mcg dose
- Ideally volume administered should be limited to 0.3mL – 0.5mL per nostril to a maximum of 1mL.
- Alternate nostrils until total dose has been delivered.
- Where possible, IN Fentanyl should be used in addition to paracetamol and an NSAID. - All patients receiving Fentanyl should be on continuous monitoring. - Fentanyl should not be administered if the maximum dose of Morphine has already been administered.
- Antiemetics should only be administered if the patient has nausea / vomiting prior to or post administration of Fentanyl.

Ipratropium.text:
##### Type
Anticholinergic bronchodilators [S4]

##### Presentations
Pressurised metered dose inhaler (pMDI) – 21mcg per activation

##### Actions

- Bronchodilation

- Blocks vagal reflexes which mediate bronchoconstriction

Onset: 3 – 5 minutes 
 Duration: 2 – 4 hours 

#### Uses

- Severe respiratory distress due to asthma or COPD

#### Adverse Effects
Rare with single use. Occasionally, urine retention.

#### Contraindications
Known hypersensitivity

#### Precautions
Care needed with use in patients with glaucoma

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Safe to use

##### Breastfeeding
- Safe to use

#### Doses#### Severe to life threatening respiratory distress
**pMDI via spacer**
##### Adult ≥6yrs
- 8 actuations 
- Repeat once after 20 minutes
- 1 actuation to 4 breaths.

##### Paediatric

Salbutamol.text:
##### Type
Synthetic \u03b22 receptor stimulant [S4]

##### Presentations
Pressurised metered dose inhaler (pMDI) – 100mcg per activation

##### Actions

- Bronchodilation

- Relaxation of involuntary muscle

- Moves K+ from extracellular to intracellular space

#### Uses

- Respiratory distress due to asthma or COPD

- Emergency treatment of suspected hyperkalaemia 

#### Adverse Effects

- Tachycardia

- Tremors

- Hypotension

#### Contraindications
Known hypersensitivity 

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Safe to use

##### Breastfeeding
- Safe to use

#### Doses#### Mild to moderate respiratory distress
**pMDI via spacer**
##### Adult ≥6 years
- 4-12 actuations 
- Repeat every 20-30 minutes
- 1 actuation to 4 breaths.
